UC-001.dor_hook: My sales pipeline lives in a spreadsheet and in my head — and a stalled proposal goes cold without anyone noticing.
UC-001.problema: My sales pipeline lives in a spreadsheet and in my head. Leads come in from everywhere — LinkedIn, referrals, email — and not all of them make it into the spreadsheet; by the next day it's already out of date and nobody fully trusts it. Follow-up depends on me remembering, and a stalled proposal goes cold without anyone noticing. At the end of the month, piecing together a picture of the pipeline is time wasted stitching fragments together. I want to stop running sales by hand: someone to log every opportunity, push the pipeline forward, chase the follow-up at the right time, and flag me when something stalls — and escalate to me only what needs a decision.
UC-001.pedido:
I run sales at a software company and I need to get organized about how I win new customers. Right now it's all manual: prospects come in through LinkedIn, referrals, and email, I jot them down in a spreadsheet (columns: name, company, source channel, what they do, the size of their own client book, status, last conversation, next step) with about 50 rows, and I lose track.

I want a system that manages my pipeline end to end. I need every prospect who comes in to become a living record; the pipeline to have clear stages (new → qualified → in conversation → proposal → closed) and to see on a board which stage each one is in; and the concepts of "company," "contact person," and "opportunity" linked together, with the history of each conversation saved. My ideal customer is a specific profile (a consultant or small service business with its own client book) — I want to be able to tag who fits that profile and prioritize them.

What kills me most is the follow-up: I want the system to remind me (or chase it itself) to follow up with the people who've gone quiet, to alert me when a proposal has been sitting too long, and when I ask "which opportunities have stalled?" to answer with the list and a suggested next step for each one. Every message that goes out in my name I want to see and approve first — nothing fires on its own. Cases that call for a decision (a nonstandard discount, a strategic client, a delicate negotiation) I want brought to me with the history ready.

Once it's running, I'll want to add: when I close a customer, automatically create the contract record and kick off their onboarding.

dentro: Linked Lead/Account/Contact/Opportunity/Activity objects + history
Visual pipeline by stage (kanban) + ICP tagging
Next-best-action agent: follow-up, stalled-proposal alerts, next step
Lightweight conversational BI ("which opportunities have stalled?")
Human approval: preview + approval of everything that goes out in the owner's name
fora: Full Contract object (that's UC-003; it enters via the roadmap)
OAuth integration with LinkedIn/an external CRM
evol: LinkedIn connector once there's an owning wave (v3)
SDR outreach agent (UC-002) layers on top

dims.t: Lead, Account, Contact, Opportunity, and Activity linked together; import from the real spreadsheet (columns → fields); pipeline-stage standardized status and ICP flag; touchpoint history per opportunity
Pipeline/kanban with the stages visible; rich opportunity record (contact, history, next step); filter by stage and by ICP
Follow-up scheduled and chased on its own; alert when a proposal sits past the threshold; on close, a hook into contract/onboarding (evolution)
Next-best-action agent over the pipeline: who to contact, when; drafts the touch, moves the stage, answers NL questions about the pipeline; escalates the hard cases to a human
Email (connected channel) for the touch and the preview; Calendar to schedule follow-up
Preview + human approval before any send; lead PII (email/phone) masked; audit trail of every agent action

UC-001.kpi: a real pipeline the app spits out — leads captured → qualified (ICP) → closed → active, with no shadow spreadsheet. In the end, you'll say: "I stopped losing opportunities to forgetfulness and I built the picture of my pipeline in seconds, not hours."

objecoes.q: Will this message my customers on its own?
objecoes.a: No. The system chases and drafts the follow-up, but you see and approve every message before it goes out.

objecoes.q: Do I have to switch tools or know how to code?
objecoes.a: No. You describe how you sell in plain conversation and import your current spreadsheet; no code.

objecoes.q: What if I want to change my pipeline stages later?
objecoes.a: You adjust it by talking to the system — stages, alerts, and fields change without waiting on IT.

UC-002.dor_hook: Too many leads come in and I can't respond in time — the good ones go cold waiting.
UC-002.problema: Too many leads come in and I can't get back to everyone in time, let alone qualify them. Some aren't even my profile and I waste time on them; others are great and go cold because I was slow to reach out. The first contact is always me writing from scratch, one by one, and in the rush a lot of people never get a reply. I want someone to make the first touch with every lead that comes in, draw out the conversation to figure out whether they fit my profile, and only bring me — with the context already in hand — the ones who are ready to move forward. But nothing can go out without me seeing it: it's my face on the message.
UC-002.pedido:
I get leads all the time over WhatsApp and through a form, and I can't give everyone a first response fast enough. I want an assistant that, the moment a new lead lands, makes first contact: introduces itself, asks 2 or 3 questions to understand what the person needs and whether they're my kind of customer (my ideal profile is a consultant or small service business with its own client book), and logs the answers on the lead's record as it goes.

Based on those answers, I want it to classify the lead as "hot / warm / not a fit" and hand me the hot ones already with a summary of what the person wants and a suggested next step. The warm ones I want it to nurture with a sequence of touches over the following days, without bothering me, until they heat up or drop off.

The rule I won't budge on: every message it's going to send in my name I want to review and approve before it goes out — at least at first. When a lead asks for something off-script (special pricing, a special term, a tough technical question), it stops and hands me the conversation with the history.

Once it's running, I'll want it to learn the tone of my best messages and suggest replies that sound more and more like me.

dentro: First touch + qualification script (questions → answers on the record)
Hot/warm/not-a-fit classification
Nurture cadence for the warm ones (multi-touch)
Human approval: preview + approval of every send; off-script escalation
fora: Discovery/scraping of new leads (the source is the UC-001 pipeline)
Closing/proposal (the owner is the one who closes)

dims.t: Lead with qualification fields (need, ICP fit, classification) linked to the UC-001 pipeline; touch history
Lead queue by classification; lead record with the answers and a summary; message preview before sending
First touch fires when a lead comes in; multi-touch nurture cadence for the warm ones with timers; stop and escalate when off-script
Agent runs the qualification through conversation, classifies, drafts the outreach in the right tone, and decides when to escalate
WhatsApp (connected channel) for the touch and the conversation
Preview + human approval of every send; lead PII masked; audit trail of every agent touch

UC-002.kpi: time to first contact with a lead drops from hours/days to minutes, and the owner only spends attention on leads that are already qualified. In the end, you'll say: "no lead goes unanswered, and I only talk to the ones worth my time." Measurable by: % of leads with a first touch < X min and % of conversations that reach the owner already qualified.

objecoes.q: Will the system talk to my lead pretending to be me?
objecoes.a: It makes the first touch and the screening questions, but you approve every message before it goes out — the face on the conversation is yours.

objecoes.q: Will it drop a good lead on its own?
objecoes.a: No. It classifies leads as hot, warm, or not a fit and hands you the hot ones with a summary; you're the one who decides to move forward.

objecoes.q: Do I need to know how to code to set up the questions?
objecoes.a: No. You describe your ideal customer and the script in plain conversation, and adjust it whenever you want.

UC-003.dor_hook: Once I close, the contract turns into a folder lost in Drive — and the renewal always catches me off guard.
UC-003.problema: Once I close a customer, the contract turns into a folder lost in Drive. I don't know off the top of my head what expires when, who has to approve a rate increase, or when it's time to renew — I find out late, after the customer has already complained or the deadline has already passed. Every renegotiation starts over from scratch because I don't keep the version history. I want one place where each contract lives with its dates, that warns me before it expires or renews, and that won't let anyone approve above their limit without going through the right person — with proof of who approved what.
UC-003.pedido:
I close contracts with my customers and right now they're left lying around: a PDF in Drive, the expiration date in my memory, and renewal time always catching me off guard. I want to get this organized.

I need each contract to be a record with: customer, amount, start date, expiration date, renewal window, and status (active, up for renewal, expired, closed). I want to attach the contract file and keep the versions — when I renegotiate, I want to see what changed from the previous version. When a contract is getting close to expiring or renewing, I want to be warned ahead of time (about 30 days out) and have the customer's history on hand to decide.

There's an important approval rule: a rate increase or discount above a certain amount can't be approved by just anyone — it has to come to me (or whoever I designate). I want it on record who approved what and when, in a way I can show later in an audit.

Once it's running, I'll want the contract to be created automatically with that customer's details whenever I close an opportunity in my sales pipeline.

dentro: Contract object with dates, status, amount, versions, and an attachment
Expiration/renewal alerts with lead time
Approval limits (rate increase/discount above the threshold → human approval)
Auditable trail of approvals (who/what/when)
fora: AI drafting/generation of the contract text
evol: Automatic contract creation on closing an opportunity (v2)

dims.t: Contract linked to the Customer/Account; dates (start, expiration, renewal), amount, status (standardized status); versioning with a diff between versions; attachment
Contract list filterable by status/expiration; contract record with version and approval history
Durable expiration/renewal workflow with early alerts; chained approval limits (above the threshold → the designated approver)
Detects what's expiring/renewing and proposes the action; builds the renewal case from the customer's history
The existing channel (connected channel) just for the expiration/renewal notices
Tamper-evident trail of approvals (who/what/when), audit-ready; the approval limit as a business object (proof + undo)

UC-003.kpi: zero expired contracts / missed renewals from forgetfulness — every expiration is flagged ahead of time and every approval is on record. In the end, you'll say: "I never miss a renewal deadline again and I can prove who approved each rate increase." Measurable by: % of renewals handled before expiration and coverage of the approval trail.

objecoes.q: Does the system sign the contract digitally?
objecoes.a: Not in this first version; it organizes dates, versions, and approvals and warns you before expiration. E-signature comes later.

objecoes.q: Can I prove later who approved each rate increase?
objecoes.a: Yes. Every approval is recorded with who, what, and when, ready to show in an audit.

objecoes.q: Do I have to migrate my contracts by hand?
objecoes.a: You attach the files and enter the key dates; from there the tracking runs on its own.

UC-004.dor_hook: I closed the sale and now a step in onboarding always slips through — the new customer hears nothing and starts to doubt.
UC-004.problema: I closed the sale and now comes the part that loses me the most customers: the beginning. Every new customer has a list of things that need to happen — request documents, schedule the kickoff meeting, set up access, send the welcome material — and it all lives in my head and in scattered emails. When a lot of them come in at once, some step slips through: the customer goes days without hearing anything, figures they've been forgotten, and the relationship starts off cold. I want a playbook that runs on its own for each new customer, shows me what's still open, and sends the right communications at the right time — without me having to remember every one of them.
UC-004.pedido:
I run a service business and I close about 8 to 12 new customers a month. Every time a customer closes, a welcome ritual kicks off that today I handle by hand: request the documents and access, book the kickoff meeting, send the welcome email, set up what they purchased, and confirm everything's live. This lives in scattered emails and in my memory, and when several come in together some step slips through — the customer hears nothing and starts to doubt.

I want the system, as soon as I add a new customer (or import from a spreadsheet with name, email, purchased plan, and start date), to open an onboarding playbook on its own with the steps in the right order and show me on a dashboard what's outstanding per customer. Every step that involves communication — welcome, document request, kickoff reminder, "your access is ready" — I want it to draft the email in my company's tone and show it to me to approve before sending; nothing goes out without my OK. If a customer gets stuck on a step for too long (a document that doesn't arrive, a kickoff not booked), I want it to flag me so I can act.

Once it's running, I'll want to add an automatic satisfaction survey at the end of onboarding and a different playbook for customers on the top-tier plan (which has more steps).

dentro: Customer + onboarding plan + steps, imported/entered
Durable step playbook with order, deadlines, and reminders
Email composed per step + preview and OK; stuck-step alert
Outstanding-items dashboard per customer
fora: Customer portal for them to track it themselves
Automatic access provisioning in an external system

dims.t: Customer linked to an onboarding plan with steps (per-step status standardized status); import of the closed-customer spreadsheet
Dashboard of onboardings in progress with outstanding items per customer; customer record with the playbook and the communication history
Durable workflow that opens the playbook when the customer is added, advances steps by deadline/dependency, and alerts when one stalls; human approval gate before each email
Drafts each step's communication in the company's tone; detects a stalled onboarding and flags the next action
Email (connected channel) for the communications; WhatsApp as an alternative channel
Nothing is sent without an OK; customer contact data protected; trail of what was sent per step

UC-004.kpi: time to a customer being fully live (from the "yes" to onboarding complete) and % of onboardings with no step forgotten. In the end, you'll say: "every new customer is welcomed the same way and on time, without me becoming the bottleneck — and I stop losing anyone at the start to silence."

objecoes.q: Do the welcome messages go out without me seeing them?
objecoes.a: No. The system drafts each step's email in your company's tone, but nothing is sent without your OK.

objecoes.q: Does it work for any customer, or is it a fixed playbook?
objecoes.a: In this version it's a standard playbook you design; different playbooks per plan come in a later evolution.

objecoes.q: Do I need IT to set up the playbook?
objecoes.a: No. You describe the steps in plain conversation and import your customer spreadsheet; no code.

UC-005.dor_hook: I only find out a customer is unhappy once they've already asked to cancel.
UC-005.problema: I notice a customer is unhappy too late — once they've already asked to cancel. I have no way to look at my book of business and know who's healthy and who's cooling off: usage, open tickets, late payments, time since we last spoke — it's all scattered. So I lose customers I could have saved if I'd acted in time, and I also miss the ones who were ready to buy more. I want a view of my book that tells me, per customer, how well the relationship is going, warns me early when someone's at risk, and suggests what to do — so I can act ahead of time instead of firefighting.
UC-005.pedido:
I have a book of about 80 recurring customers at a service business. My problem is that I only find out a customer is unhappy when they tell me they're going to cancel — when it's already too late. The signals are there, but they're scattered: I haven't spoken to them in a while, they've opened several tickets, they were late on the last payment, they used less than they signed up for. I can't look at the whole book and say who's doing well and who's at risk.

I want a dashboard of my book that gives me, per customer, a relationship health s (green / watch / at-risk) combining those signals, and that explains to me why the s is what it is. When a customer goes at-risk, I want the system to warn me on its own and suggest the next step (a call, a re-engagement email, a term), and to trigger a retention playbook that I approve before each contact — nothing goes to the customer without my OK. Likewise, when a customer is very healthy and using it to the hilt, I want it flagged to me as an opportunity to sell more. I'll start by entering my customers and the signals in a spreadsheet (customer, plan, last contact, open tickets, payment status, usage this month).

Once it's running, I'll want to connect this to the system where my customers actually use the product, so the health s reads real usage instead of me typing it in; and I want to be able to ask in plain language things like "which at-risk customers are on the premium plan?"

dentro: Customer + signals (usage, tickets, payment, last contact) imported from a spreadsheet
Per-customer health s (green/watch/at-risk) with an explanation
Churn alert + retention playbook with a human approval gate per contact
Expansion (upsell) signal + a plain-language question about the book
fora: Automatic reading of the customer's product usage (external system of record)
evol: Bridge to UC-007 (collections) for the late-payment case

dims.t: Customer linked to the signals (usage, tickets, payment, contact) with history; spreadsheet import; health-band standardized status
**Book dashboard with the health s aggregated by band + a per-account cockpit with the reason for the s and the history**
**Durable retention playbook that fires when a customer goes at-risk and advances through contacts over time, with a human approval gate before each one**
**Computes the health s by combining the signals, explains the s, suggests the next-best-action (retain/expand), and alerts before churn**
Email/WhatsApp (connected channel) for the retention contacts; NL querying over the book (conversational BI)
Customer data protected + role-based permissions by CS role; nothing is sent without an OK; trail of the retention actions

UC-005.kpi: churn avoided (at-risk customers recovered) and expansion generated (a flagged upsell that closed). In the end, you'll say: "I stopped losing customers by surprise — I see the risk early and act; and I found out who was ready to buy more."

objecoes.q: Does it contact my at-risk customer on its own?
objecoes.a: No. The system flags the risk and suggests the next step, but every retention contact goes through your OK before it goes out.

objecoes.q: Do I need to connect my product-usage system?
objecoes.a: Not in this version; you start with a spreadsheet of signals (usage, tickets, payment). Reading real usage automatically is an evolution.

objecoes.q: Is the health s a black box?
objecoes.a: No. It shows you why the s is green, watch, or at-risk, with the signals that weighed in.

UC-006.dor_hook: WhatsApp support swallows my team, and after hours the customer is left hanging.
UC-006.problema: My support team is always firefighting. Messages come in all day over WhatsApp, one person answers everything by hand, and after hours the customer is left hanging — by morning the queue has already piled up. A lot of the questions are the same ones as always and still take up someone's time. What's urgent gets mixed in with what can wait, and sometimes the important case blows its deadline because nobody saw it. I want the repetitive volume handled on its own, right in the conversation, and only the exceptions or the urgent cases reaching a person — without the customer having to repeat their story.
UC-006.pedido:
I serve my customers mostly over WhatsApp and the volume swallows me. A lot of the messages are repeat questions (status, common question, simple request) that could be handled on the spot, but they depend on someone on the team answering one by one. After hours nobody answers and the customer sits waiting.

I want support that handles the volume on its own and frees up my team for what matters. When a message comes in, support should understand what the person wants, answer the common questions, and resolve what can be resolved in the conversation. Every contact becomes a ticket with a status (open, in progress, resolved) and a priority. Each type of ticket has a deadline to be resolved — if that deadline is about to blow, I want an alert, and the urgent ones have to jump the queue.

Whatever support can't resolve, or anything that's a complaint, an urgent matter, or a delicate case, has to be handed to a person on my team — with the full conversation history on screen, so nobody has to ask again for what the customer already said. I want to see, on a dashboard, how many tickets are open, which ones are close to blowing their deadline, and what's already been resolved.

Once it's running, I'll want to add an automatic satisfaction survey when each ticket closes.

dentro: Ticket with status, priority, SLA by type
Agent resolves the common questions and whatever it can in the conversation
Escalation to a human with the history (exception/urgent/complaint)
Queue dashboard: open, close to breaching SLA, resolved
fora: Rich knowledge base (RAG) with versioned sources

dims.t: Ticket linked to the Customer; status and priority standardized status; ticket type with an SLA; conversation history
Support queue filterable (status, priority, SLA); ticket record with the conversation; queue dashboard
SLA timer by type with an alert on breach; prioritization (urgent jumps the queue); escalation to a human
Agent understands the request, answers/resolves the common volume in the conversation, and decides what to escalate
WhatsApp (connected channel) as the support channel
Trail of every interaction (who asked, what was done, when); customer PII masked; escalation preserves the context

UC-006.kpi: % of volume resolved without touching a person on the team + zero urgent tickets breaching SLA without an alert. In the end, you'll say: "the repetitive stuff disappears from my team's queue and we only handle what actually needs a person." Measurable by: self-resolution rate and % of SLAs met.

objecoes.q: Will the bot answer just anything and embarrass me in front of my customer?
objecoes.a: It only resolves what it knows; a complaint, an urgent matter, or a delicate case it hands to a person on the team with the full history.

objecoes.q: Will the customer notice it's not a person?
objecoes.a: Support answers in your tone and resolves the request; whatever needs a person reaches you without the customer having to repeat their story.

objecoes.q: Do I have to change my WhatsApp number?
objecoes.a: No. Support runs on your usual channel and you configure it by conversation, no code.

UC-007.dor_hook: The due date passes and nobody notices in time.
UC-007.problema: My invoices live in a spreadsheet and collections happen by hand: when I remember, I send a WhatsApp one at a time. The due date passes and nobody notices in time, and collecting means composing the right message, in the right tone, customer by customer — which is a drag and drags on. The dunning sequence (reminder, collection, renegotiation) exists in theory; in practice it's manual. With no organized history, every collection starts over from scratch. I'm losing money to forgetfulness. I want something that tracks the due dates, composes the collection at the right stage and in the right tone, and shows it to me before sending — nothing goes out without my OK.
UC-007.pedido:
I run a B2B service business with about 60 active customers. The invoices sit in a spreadsheet (columns: customer, tax ID, amount, due date, status) and collections happen by hand: when I remember, I send a WhatsApp one at a time. A customer goes past due and nobody notices in time; renegotiations have no history.

I want to stop losing money to forgetfulness. I need my invoice and customer spreadsheet to import as-is (no retyping) and the system to track the due dates on its own. When a due date is approaching or has passed, I want it to compose the right message in the right tone for the stage (a friendly reminder beforehand → a collection notice when it's overdue → a renegotiation offer if it drags on) and show me the preview before sending — nothing goes out without my OK. I want to be able to choose the tone, firmer or more cordial.

Delicate cases — a large amount, a strategic customer, a request for a discount or renegotiation — I don't want the system to handle: I want it to prepare the case with the history ready and hand it to me to decide. And since it's money and it's my customer's tax ID, I want the sensitive data protected and every action (what was sent, when, to whom) on record, in a way I can undo if I make a mistake.

Once it's running, I'll want to see on a dashboard how much I've already recovered and how much is still outstanding by dunning stage.

dentro: Invoice + Customer imported from the spreadsheet; tax ID detected and protected
Durable 3-stage dunning sequence (reminder → collection → renegotiation) with timers
Message composed per stage + tone choice; preview + OK
Escalation of delicate cases with the history; trail + undo
fora: Issuing a payment slip/instant payment/tax invoice
evol: Reconciliation with the bank/ERP (v3, system connectors)
Recovered vs. outstanding dashboard by stage (v2)

dims.t: Invoice linked to the Customer; import of the real spreadsheet; dunning-stage and status standardized status; tax ID detection/masking
List of due dates by stage; preview of each message with a tone choice; on-screen approval
Durable workflow that tracks the due dates and advances the stages with timers; human approval gate before each send
Composes the message in the right tone and stage; decides when a case is one to escalate (large amount/strategic)
WhatsApp (connected channel) for the send; email as an alternative channel
Nothing fires without an OK; PII (tax ID) masked; trail of every action; undo

UC-007.kpi: $ recovered that had been sitting there from forgetfulness — the number shows up on the dashboard (recovered vs. outstanding by stage). In the end, you'll say: "I recovered $X that was going to rot in the spreadsheet, without hiring anyone and without collecting by hand."

objecoes.q: Does the collection go out to my customer on its own?
objecoes.a: No. The system composes the message in the right tone and stage, shows you the preview, and nothing is sent without your OK.

objecoes.q: Will it embarrass my customer or dun someone who doesn't owe?
objecoes.a: You approve every message before it goes out and you choose the tone; delicate cases like a large amount or a strategic customer come to you to decide.

objecoes.q: Does it mark the invoice paid when the customer pays?
objecoes.a: In this version marking it paid is on you; automatic reconciliation with the bank is an evolution. The v1 focus is not letting a due date slip by unnoticed.

UC-008.dor_hook: Purchasing here is a game of telephone: I overpay and I can't say who approved what.
UC-008.problema: Purchasing at my company is a game of telephone: someone needs something, asks over WhatsApp or in passing, I approve it (or forget to approve it), someone issues the order, and when the goods arrive nobody checks whether what came in is what was ordered at the agreed price. The result: I buy things I didn't need, I overpay, and I have no way to show who approved what. Above a certain amount it should go through me, but that depends on people remembering. I want every purchase to follow a path: someone requests, whoever has the authority approves according to the amount, it becomes an order, and at receiving we check against what was ordered — all on record, so I know where my money is going.
UC-008.pedido:
I have a company with about 25 employees and purchasing is controlled chaos. Whoever needs something asks informally, approval depends on me remembering, and when the goods arrive nobody checks whether they came in right and at the agreed price. I buy too much, I overpay, and I have no record of who approved what.

I want to organize this as a pipeline. Anyone opens a requisition (what they need, quantity, what for, a suggested vendor, an estimated amount). Depending on the amount, it goes to whoever has the authority to approve — up to $500 the area lead, above that me; if it goes over $5,000, I want a second sign-off. Once approved, it becomes a purchase order that goes out to the vendor by email. When the goods arrive, whoever receives them logs the receipt and the system checks it against the order — do the quantity and amount match? If they don't, it warns me. I want a dashboard of what's at each stage (requested, approving, purchased, received) and the record of who approved each thing.

Once it's running, I'll want a registry of approved vendors (only buy from ones that are approved) and an alert when I'm buying a lot of an item — so I can negotiate better.

dentro: Requisition → Order → Receipt as linked objects; stage standardized status
Approval authority by amount (chained above a ceiling), with a human approval gate
Receipt-vs-order check (quantity/amount) with a discrepancy alert
Dashboard by stage + trail of who approved
fora: Tax-document issuing / integration with a financial ERP
evol: Posting and reconciliation in the ERP via system connectors (v2)
Alert on recurring spend by item (v2)

dims.t: **Requisition → Order → Receipt as a linked graph, with an integrity rule (what's received matches what was ordered — a lightweight 3-way match)**; Vendor and Item; stage standardized status
Requisition queue by stage with filters; order record with line items and approval history
**Durable requisition→approval→order→receipt workflow with authority chained by amount and a human approval gate at each approval**
Helps build the requisition, suggests a vendor from history, flags off-policy purchases and receiving discrepancies
Email (connected channel) to send the order to the vendor; WhatsApp as an alternative
role-based permissions by role (requester/approver/receiver); trail of every approval; nothing gets bought without going through the authority

UC-008.kpi: % of purchases within policy (with approval recorded at the correct authority level) and receiving discrepancies caught before payment. In the end, you'll say: "every purchase follows the right path, I know who approved it, and I don't pay for goods that didn't arrive the way I ordered."

objecoes.q: Can just anyone approve a purchase?
objecoes.a: No. Every purchase follows the authority-by-amount rule you define; above the ceiling it goes up to whoever has approval power, and everything is on record.

objecoes.q: Does it issue tax documents or integrate with my ERP?
objecoes.a: Not in this version; it organizes the requisition, approval, order, and receipt. Posting to the financial ERP is an evolution.

objecoes.q: Do I need to know how to program the authority rules?
objecoes.a: No. You describe the limits by amount in conversation and adjust them whenever you want, no code.

UC-009.dor_hook: Reimbursement here is a photo of a receipt over WhatsApp and good faith — if an audit hits, I can't prove a thing.
UC-009.problema: Reimbursement here is a spreadsheet, a photo of a receipt over WhatsApp, and good faith. The employee spends, sends the photo of the receipt, I approve it on sight, and someone pays — but I have no way to know whether the receipt is real, whether it's already been reimbursed before, or whether it's within policy. At the end of the month I can't close out what was spent, and if an audit hits I can't prove each reimbursement with the right receipt. I want every expense to come in with the receipt attached, go through approval by whoever has the authority, and stay on record in a way I can prove later — no photo lost in a chat.
UC-009.pedido:
I have a team of about 20 employees who pay out of pocket and ask for reimbursement: travel, supplies, a client lunch. Today it's a mess — a photo of a receipt over WhatsApp, a spreadsheet, and me approving on sight. I don't know whether the receipt is legit, whether it's already been paid before, or whether it's within what the company allows. At the end of the month I can't close out the spend and, if I need to prove a reimbursement, I have to hunt down the photo.

I want every expense logged with the receipt attached (amount, date, category, what for, and the photo/receipt). The system should read the receipt and fill in what it can (amount, date), check it against policy (a cap per category, requires a justification above a certain amount) and warn if it's out of bounds. Then it goes to approval by whoever has the authority — the person's manager, and above $300 the finance team too. Once approved, it enters the payment queue. I want a dashboard of what's pending approval and pending payment, and each reimbursement to keep the receipt and the record of who approved it forever, with date and time, in a way that holds up for an audit — with no way for me to "lose" anything later.

Once it's running, I'll want an alert for a duplicate receipt (the same receipt logged twice) and a monthly close that rolls everything up by cost center.

dentro: Expense with the receipt attached + reading what it can (amount/date)
Policy check (cap per category) + approval authority with human approval
Pending queue (approval/payment)
Tamper-evident trail: receipt + who approved + when
fora: Integration with payroll/accounting for the payment

dims.t: Expense linked to an Employee and a Category, with the receipt attached; status standardized status; cap per category (policy)
Expense queue by status with filters; expense record with the receipt and the approval history
Flow of log→policy check→approval (authority)→payment with a human approval gate; multi-step with deadlines
Reads the receipt and fills in amount/date; checks against policy and flags an expense over the cap
Email (connected channel) for approval/payment notices
**Tamper-evident trail: receipt + approver + date/time immutable (proof for an audit); employee PII protected; role-based permissions by role**

UC-009.kpi: % of reimbursements with a traceable receipt and approval (100% auditable) and off-policy spend caught before payment. In the end, you'll say: "every reimbursement has a receipt and an approval owner; if an audit hits, I can prove every cent — and I stop paying for what's out of policy."

objecoes.q: Does it pay the reimbursement automatically?
objecoes.a: No. It organizes the entry, checks policy, and takes it to approval by whoever has the authority; payment stays with you in this version.

objecoes.q: Can I prove each reimbursement in an audit?
objecoes.a: Yes. The receipt, who approved it, and the date and time are recorded in a way you can't erase later.

objecoes.q: Does it catch a receipt logged twice?
objecoes.a: The duplicate-receipt alert comes in a later evolution; v1 already attaches and keeps each receipt with your approval.

UC-010.dor_hook: I spend hours a week putting together status because the client has no way to see for themselves where their project stands.
UC-010.problema: I run several client projects at once and the biggest source of friction isn't the work — it's the client not knowing where things stand. They message me "hey, how's it going?" every week, and I drop everything to put together a status. Internally I lose track too: who's doing what, what's behind, what's waiting on a reply from the client. I want one place where my team tracks the deliverables on each project and where the client themselves can see the progress on what's theirs — without me becoming the weekly status messenger.
UC-010.pedido:
I run an agency handling about 15 client projects at once. My chaos is twofold: internally I lose track of who's doing what and what's behind; and externally I spend hours a week putting together a status for every client who asks "how's my project going?"

I want to organize each project with its deliverables and stages (to do / in progress / in review / done), with an owner and a deadline, and an internal dashboard that shows me, across all projects, what's behind and what's waiting on a reply from the client. And I want each client to have a way to see the progress on just their project — the deliverables, what's ready, what depends on them — without me having to build a report and without them seeing the other clients' projects. When a deliverable is stuck waiting on the client, I want them to be notified.

I'll start by entering the projects and deliverables (or importing from a spreadsheet with project, deliverable, owner, deadline, status). Once it's running, I'll want the client to be able to approve a deliverable right in their view, and an automatic weekly status summary.

dentro: Project → Deliverable → Stage as linked objects; spreadsheet import; status standardized status
Internal cross-project dashboard (behind / waiting on client)
**The client's view of their own project**
Notice to the client when a deliverable is waiting on them
fora: Deliverable approval by the client in their view

dims.t: Project linked to Deliverables and Stages with an owner and a deadline; Client as the project owner; status standardized status
**Internal cross-project board/timeline with filters + a public surface where the client sees only the progress on their own project (public page)**
Deliverable workflow that advances through stages, marks lateness, and flags when it's waiting on the client; reminders with a timer
Flags deliverables at risk/behind and what's stuck waiting on the client (support, not autonomous operation)
Email/WhatsApp (connected channel) to notify the client; the public view is public page
**Row-level isolation: each client sees only their own project, never the others'; trail of status changes; internal role-based permissions by role**

UC-010.kpi: hours/week saved putting together status + a drop in deliverables stalled for lack of a client reply (the client sees and responds on their own). In the end, you'll say: "I stopped being the status messenger — the client tracks it themselves and the deliverables no longer stall waiting on them."

objecoes.q: Will the client see the other clients' projects?
objecoes.a: No. Each client sees only the progress on their own project, never the others'.

objecoes.q: Can the client already track it themselves today?
objecoes.a: The client view depends on a public page that's on the platform roadmap; until it ships, the team uses the internal dashboard and the client is notified by message.

objecoes.q: Do I have to set it all up by hand?
objecoes.a: You import your project and deliverable spreadsheet and adjust the stages in conversation; no code.

UC-011.dor_hook: I open a role and drown in résumés — and I end up giving almost nobody a reply.
UC-011.problema: When I open a role, résumés pour in and I drown. I read them one by one in my email, reply to a few, forget to get back to most (which burns my brand), and the ones who advance turn into a jumble of "who have I already talked to, who did which interview, who am I waiting on." A good candidate goes cold because I was slow to reply. I want the first screen of the résumés done for me — sorting out who fits the role — and the ones who advance to enter an organized interview pipeline, with the candidates knowing where they stand.
UC-011.pedido:
I run a company that hires about 3 to 5 people a month and recruiting eats me alive. I open the role, get dozens of résumés by email, read them by hand, and can't get back to everyone — which sends a terrible message. The candidates who advance turn into a mess: I don't know who I've already talked to, who did which interview, who I'm waiting on.

I want the system, for each role (with the description and requirements I define), to do the first screen of the résumés that come in: read them, compare them against the requirements, and sort out for me the ones who fit, with a summary of why — without dropping anyone definitively on its own; the decision to advance is mine. The ones I approve enter a pipeline by stage (screened / HR interview / technical interview / offer / hired), and I want a dashboard to see where each candidate is. I want the system to send the interview-invite emails and, importantly, the replies to those who didn't advance — always with my OK first. I'll start by entering a role and dropping in a few résumés.

Once it's running, I'll want it to propose interview times by looking at my calendar and a talent pool to reuse good candidates for future roles.

dentro: Role → Candidate → Stage as linked objects; pipeline-stage standardized status
Screening by agent: reads the résumé, compares against the requirements, summarizes and ranks
Interview pipeline with a dashboard + emails (invite and reply) with human approval
evol: Public role / sourcing via public page

dims.t: Role with requirements linked to Candidates and Stages; stage standardized status; résumé attached to the candidate
Candidate pipeline/kanban by stage with filters; candidate record with the résumé, the screening summary, and history
Pipeline that advances through stages with deadlines/reminders; human approval gate before each email (invite and reply)
Agent reads the résumé, compares it against the role's requirements, summarizes and ranks; never drops anyone definitively on its own
Email (connected channel) for invites and replies; Calendar for interviews (evolution)
Candidate PII (ID number/personal data) masked before the LLM; role-based permissions by role (HR/manager); pipeline trail

UC-011.kpi: screening hours saved per role + % of candidates who get a reply (employer brand). In the end, you'll say: "I don't drown in résumés anymore, I reply to everyone, and the good candidate doesn't go cold because I was slow."

objecoes.q: Does the system drop a candidate on its own?
objecoes.a: No. It reads the résumés, compares them against the requirements, and sorts out for you the ones who fit with a summary; the decision to advance or drop is yours.

objecoes.q: Do the reply emails go out without me seeing them?
objecoes.a: No. Invites and replies are drafted for you, but nothing is sent without your OK — including the reply to those who didn't advance.

objecoes.q: What about bias in the screening?
objecoes.a: The screening is a support that shows you the reason for the recommendation; the candidate's personal data is protected and a person always decides.

UC-012.dor_hook: On the way in, something's always missing; on the way out, the ex-employee's access stays live for weeks.
UC-012.problema: When someone joins, it's a scramble between HR, IT, and the manager — contract, system access, equipment, introductions — and something is always missing: the person shows up on day one with no computer or no email access. When someone leaves it's worse and more dangerous: sometimes the ex-employee's access stays active for weeks because nobody told IT to cut it. Each area does its own part in isolation, nobody sees the whole, and I have no way to guarantee everything got done. I want a playbook that, when I record a hire or a departure, hands out the tasks to HR, IT, and the manager and shows me that each one is complete — above all, cutting access on the way out.
UC-012.pedido:
I have a company with about 40 employees and people joining and leaving is always a headache. On a hire, HR handles the contract, IT the access and the equipment, and the manager the onboarding — but everyone works in isolation and something is always missing: the person shows up with no computer or no email. On a departure it's worse: sometimes the ex-employee's access stays active for weeks because nobody told IT, and that's a security risk.

I want the system, when I record a hire (name, role, manager, start date) or a departure (who, date, reason), to open the right playbook on its own and hand out the tasks to each area: HR does its part, IT does its (create/cut access, equipment), the manager does theirs. Each person sees only the queue of their own tasks, with a deadline, and I see a dashboard of the whole — what's left for the person to be 100% ready on a hire, and above all, on a departure, that every access was actually cut by the date. If a critical task (cut access) goes past deadline, I want a strong alert. Some tasks depend on others (I only hand over the laptop after the agreement is signed) — respect the order.

Once it's running, I'll want different playbooks by role type (a remote hire doesn't get a badge) and an equipment-return checklist on departure.

dentro: A hire/departure opens a playbook with tasks handed out by area (HR/IT/manager)
Queue by role + whole-picture dashboard; dependencies between tasks; status standardized status
Strong alert for a late critical task (cutting access on departure)
Completion trail for each task
fora: Automatic provisioning/cutting of access in the systems

dims.t: Employee linked to a Playbook (hire/departure) with Tasks by area and dependencies; status standardized status
**Multiple roles (HR/IT/manager), each with their own task queue + a consolidated dashboard of what's left per person**
**Durable cross-area workflow that hands out the tasks, respects the dependencies between them, chases deadlines, and escalates a late critical task**
Generates the right playbook by type (hire/departure) and flags lateness/risk (support, not autonomous operation)
Email (connected channel) to notify each owner
role-based permissions by role; completion trail (proof that access was cut on departure); employee PII protected

UC-012.kpi: % of hires 100% ready on day one and time to full access cutoff on departure (security risk zeroed out). In the end, you'll say: "nobody shows up without access anymore, and no ex-employee is left with the door open — each area does its part and I see the whole."

objecoes.q: Does it cut the access in the systems on its own?
objecoes.a: In this version it generates and chases the cutoff task and alerts you if it's late, but IT is still the one who executes it in the system. Automatic execution is an evolution.

objecoes.q: Does each area see everyone's queue?
objecoes.a: No. Each person sees only their own tasks; you see the whole-picture dashboard — what's left for each hire and whether every access was cut on departure.

objecoes.q: Do I need an HR system to use it?
objecoes.a: No. You describe the join and leave playbooks in conversation, no code and no switching tools.

UC-013.dor_hook: My techs are out in the field and the information vanishes — the customer calls "when's he coming?" and nobody knows.
UC-013.problema: I have techs out in the field all day and the operation lives on WhatsApp and in the dispatcher's head. A job comes in, someone decides who goes, and then it vanishes: did the tech go? Did he do it? What did he find? The customer calls asking when he's coming and nobody can say. When the job is done, I have no proof of what was done — no photo, no signature — and then comes the complaint and the argument. I want to dispatch the jobs in an organized way, the tech to log on his phone what he did with a photo, and the customer to be able to track when the tech is on the way and see that the job was completed.
UC-013.pedido:
I run a service business with techs who work at the customer's home/business (maintenance, installation). Today it's all loose WhatsApp: the job comes in, someone picks the tech off the top of their head, and then the information vanishes — I don't know whether the tech went, what he did, and I have no proof of the job. The customer keeps calling "when's he coming?" and nobody can answer.

I want to receive the jobs (customer, address, problem, priority) and dispatch them to a tech, who gets the work order on WhatsApp with the job details. The tech should be able, from his phone, to say he's on the way, that he's arrived, what he did, and attach a before/after photo and the customer's signature or OK to close the order. On my end I want a dashboard of the jobs by status (open, dispatched, in service, completed) and who's running late. And I want the customer to be able to track their own job — "tech on the way," "completed" — without seeing anyone else's jobs. Every completed order has to keep the evidence (photos, timestamp, customer OK) so I can defend against a complaint.

Once it's running, I'll want the system to suggest the nearest/available tech and send a satisfaction survey to the customer right after the close.

dentro: Work order linked to a Customer and a Tech; status standardized status; evidence (photo/OK) attached
Dispatch + the tech logs on WhatsApp (on the way/arrived/done) + close with evidence
**The customer tracking their own job**
Internal dashboard by status + lateness
fora: Parts inventory used on the job
Scheduling with the customer picking a time
evol: Deducting a part from inventory (v2, bridges UC-014)

dims.t: Work order linked to a Customer, a Tech, and Evidence (photos/OK); status and priority standardized status
**Internal dispatch board + the tech's field interaction (WhatsApp/phone) + customer tracking (public page)**
Order cycle (dispatch→on the way→service→close) with SLA/deadline and a lateness alert
Flags late jobs and prioritizes the queue (dispatch support; nearest-tech suggestion is an evolution)
WhatsApp (connected channel) for the tech and customer notices; public tracking via public page
**Row-level isolation: each customer sees only their own job; photo evidence + timestamp + OK as immutable proof; order trail**

UC-013.kpi: % of orders with complete evidence at close (proof against a complaint) + a drop in "where's the tech?" calls (the customer tracks it themselves). In the end, you'll say: "I know where each tech is, every order closes with a photo and the customer's OK, and I stop wasting time on the customer calling to check the status."

objecoes.q: Does the customer see other customers' jobs?
objecoes.a: No. Each customer tracks only their own job — 'tech on the way,' 'completed' — never anyone else's.

objecoes.q: Can the customer already track it today?
objecoes.a: Customer tracking depends on a public page that's on the platform roadmap; until then the team uses the internal dashboard and the tech updates over WhatsApp.

objecoes.q: How am I protected against a complaint?
objecoes.a: Every order closes with the evidence kept — photos, timestamp, and the customer's OK — in a way that can't be altered later.

UC-014.dor_hook: My inventory lives in a spreadsheet that never adds up — an item runs out and I only find out when I go to use it.
UC-014.problema: My inventory lives in a spreadsheet that's never right. When I go to sell or use something, the item is out and I didn't know; then I buy in a rush, overpay, or lose the sale. I have no idea when to restock each thing — I'm just guessing. I want the system to know how much I have of each item, warn me when it's getting low (before it runs out), and tell me it's time to restock, so I'm not caught off guard or stuck with stock sitting idle.
UC-014.pedido:
I run a small business with about 200 items in stock (products and supplies) and I track it in a spreadsheet that never adds up. The problem is always the same: I find out an item is out only when I go to use it, then I buy in a rush and overpay; or, on the other side, I pile up idle stock that doesn't move.

I want to enter my items (or import from the spreadsheet: item, current quantity, unit, vendor, reorder point) and have a list that shows how much I have of each. I want to log ins and outs so the quantity stays current. And I want the system to keep an eye out: when an item hits its reorder point, it warns me (WhatsApp/email) before it runs out and already suggests how much to restock and from which vendor, looking at consumption. A dashboard of what's in the red (below the reorder point) would help me act all at once.

Once it's running, I'll want it to generate the purchase requisition for the red items on its own (for me to just approve) and an alert for an item that's been sitting idle for a long time.

dentro: Item + Movement (in/out) + Vendor; spreadsheet import; reorder point per item
Agent monitors the level, alerts when it hits the reorder point, and suggests a restock
Dashboard of the red items
fora: Automatic reading of sales/consumption from a POS/ERP

dims.t: Item linked to Movements and a Vendor; current quantity derived from the movements; reorder point per item; spreadsheet import
Item list with the level and filters (focus on what's in the red); item record with movement history
Continuous level monitoring that triggers an alert and a restock suggestion when it crosses the reorder point
Agent tracks consumption, anticipates a stockout, and suggests how much / from whom to restock
WhatsApp/email (connected channel) for the restock alerts
Internal inventory data; movement trail (in/out traceable); single-org, low PII

UC-014.kpi: stockouts avoided (items that didn't run short because the alert came in time) + a drop in expensive emergency buying. In the end, you'll say: "I'm not caught off guard by an item running out anymore, and I stop buying in a rush at a premium."

objecoes.q: Does it update inventory on its own by pulling from sales?
objecoes.a: In this version you log ins and outs (or import the spreadsheet) and the system keeps the level and warns you at the reorder point. Pulling straight from the register is an evolution.

objecoes.q: Does it already buy what's running short on its own?
objecoes.a: It warns you and suggests how much to restock and from which vendor; automatic order generation comes later. You're the one who decides to buy.

objecoes.q: Do I have to install anything or know how to code?
objecoes.a: No. You import your item spreadsheet and adjust the reorder point in conversation; no code.

UC-015.dor_hook: My records are a dumpster fire: the same customer shows up three times and I send the same thing twice.
UC-015.problema: My customer records are a dumpster fire: the same customer shows up three times spelled differently, there are records with no tax ID, no phone, with stale data. So I send the same collection notice twice to the same customer, my reports don't add up, and nobody trusts the database. Every time I try to clean it up by hand, it's endless and someone deletes the wrong record. I want the system to find the duplicates for me, fill in what it can, and let me approve each merge before it touches anything — so I have a reliable database with no risk of losing good information.
UC-015.pedido:
My customer/company database is a mess: the same customer entered several times with different spellings, records with no tax ID or no phone, stale data. It costs me dearly — I send the same thing twice to the same customer, my numbers don't line up, and nobody trusts the database. When I try to fix it by hand it's endless, and there's always the risk of deleting the right record by mistake.

I want to import my database (a spreadsheet with name, tax ID/ID number, email, phone, address) and have the system do the cleanup for me. First, find the likely duplicates (the same customer spelled differently) and show them to me side by side, telling me how confident it is; I approve the merge before anything happens — nothing is merged on its own — and the system keeps the history of what it merged, so I can undo it if it's wrong. Second, point out the incomplete or inconsistent records (a malformed tax ID, an invalid email) and, where it can, complete/standardize them, always with my OK. In the end I want a database where each customer is a single record, reliable, with a record of everything that was changed.

Once it's running, I'll want it to keep watching the database and warn me when a new duplicate or bad record comes in, and a rule that blocks a record with no tax ID.

dentro: Database import; detection of likely duplicates with a confidence s
**Merge with human approval approval + history + undo**
Flag/standardize an incomplete or inconsistent record, with an OK
Consolidated database (1 record per customer) + change trail
fora: Enrichment from an external source (a government registry/bureau)
Continuous watching of the database in production
Entry-blocking rule (won't let you save without a tax ID)

dims.t: **Customer master record with merge/version history and integrity rules (dedup, valid tax ID/ID number) — one golden record per entity**
Queue of candidate duplicates with a side-by-side comparison; master-record view with merge history
Flow of detection→merge proposal→approval (human approval)→consolidation; standardization with a human gate
Agent does the fuzzy matching of duplicates, assigns a confidence s, proposes the merge and standardization; the human decides
Email (connected channel) for notices; external enrichment is an evolution (system connectors)
**A merge is destructive over PII across the whole database → mandatory approval + trail of what was merged + undo; PII masked before the LLM**

UC-015.kpi: duplicates eliminated and % of the database complete/valid (record reliability). In the end, you'll say: "my database became a reliable source — each customer is a single one, with complete data, and I stop sending duplicate things and seeing reports that don't add up."

objecoes.q: Will it merge records and make me lose information?
objecoes.a: No. It shows you the likely duplicates side by side and nothing is merged without your OK; if it's wrong, you can undo it, because it keeps what it merged.

objecoes.q: Does it fill in the data on its own from an external source?
objecoes.a: In this version it standardizes and points out what's incomplete in your own database; pulling data from an external source like a government registry is an evolution.

objecoes.q: Do I have to program the duplicate rules?
objecoes.a: No. You import the spreadsheet and review the proposed merges; the intelligence finds the candidates and you approve.

UC-016.dor_hook: To learn a simple number about my business, I depend on someone building a spreadsheet — and by the time it arrives, it's already changed.
UC-016.problema: I have my business's data, but to answer a simple question — "how much did I sell per rep this month?", "which customers owe me the most?" — I depend on someone building a spreadsheet or on me digging through a report. The answer takes time, and by the time it arrives it's already changed. I want to be able to ask the way I think, in plain English, and get the number and the chart on the spot, without knowing my way around a pivot table or waiting on the "data people." And I want to be able to save the questions I ask every week so they become a dashboard I open and it's already there.
UC-016.pedido:
I run a service business and my operational data (sales, customers, receivables) lives scattered. I'll start by loading a spreadsheet with my sales (columns: date, rep, customer, amount, product, payment status) and another with my customers.

What trips me up is answering day-to-day questions about that data. Today, to learn "how much each rep did this month," "who are my 10 biggest customers," or "how much do I have overdue in receivables," someone has to build a spreadsheet by hand and that takes time. I want to be able to ask these things in plain English, the way I think, and get the answer with the number and a chart on the spot — without having to know how to build a pivot table. When the answer is a list (e.g., the customers who are past due), I want to be able to export it.

The questions I ask every week I want to be able to pin to a dashboard, so I open it and see it already up to date, without redoing the question. And I want to trust the number is right: if it gives me a total, I want to be able to see where it came from.

Once it's running, I'll want to get an automatic summary by email every Monday with the indicators I pinned — without having to log in to ask.

dentro: Plain-language question over the loaded data; answer with a number + chart
Export a result as a list; see where the number came from (traceability)
Pin questions to a dashboard that opens up to date
fora: Automatic ingestion from an external system (ERP/live spreadsheet)
Advanced data modeling / heavy joins defined by the user
Alerts when an indicator crosses a threshold
evol: External data source via system connectors (v2)
Rich semantic model and named calculated metrics (v2)
Scheduled email summary of the pinned indicators (v2)

dims.t: Understands the model of the loaded data (sales, customers) and the relationships between them to answer correctly; real spreadsheet import
**An NL question surface + a generated chart + a dashboard of pinned questions (dashboard with aggregation)**; list export
On-demand query; (evolution) scheduled summary — little durable automation in v1
Translates the plain-language question into the right query over the model and picks the right visualization; explains where the number came from
None in v1 — operates over the internal loaded data (connecting to an external source is an evolution)
role-based permissions over what each role can ask; PII masked in the results; number traceability (where it came from)

UC-016.kpi: time to an answer to a business question (from hours/days waiting on someone to build a spreadsheet down to seconds) + number of questions answered without depending on a third party. In the end, you'll say: "I ask and I have the number on the spot, without depending on anyone to build a report."

UC-017.dor_hook: My vendors' certificates expire without anyone seeing it, and sometimes I sign with one who has a compliance issue.
UC-017.problema: I buy from a ton of vendors and I have no control over who's eligible. The certificates and documents sit in an old email or a folder, expire without anyone noticing, and sometimes I find out too late that I signed with one who had a compliance issue. I have no single place that says "these are the approved vendors, with their paperwork in order," nor a way to rate who delivers well and who gives me a headache — that memory lives in people's heads. I want a vendor registry that chases the documents, warns me before they expire, only lets me buy from ones that are approved, and keeps a s of how each one is doing.
UC-017.pedido:
I run a company that works with about 40 vendors and today I have no control over who's eligible to supply. The documents (articles of incorporation, certificates, licenses) are scattered, expire without anyone seeing it, and I sometimes buy from one who has a compliance issue without knowing. I'll start by entering my vendors in a spreadsheet (legal name, tax ID, category, contact, documents I have).

I want to organize this as an approval process. Every new vendor goes through a check: they (or someone on my team) send in the documents required for their category, someone reviews and approves, and only then does the vendor become "approved" and cleared for me to buy from. Each document has an expiration date — I want the system to keep an eye on it and warn me, say, 30 days before it expires, chasing the renewal from the vendor on its own, and to automatically downgrade anyone who lets it lapse.

I also want to rate who delivers well: after each supply, log a s (timeliness, quality, price) and see each vendor's history — so when it's time to choose I know who's worth it. And I want a dashboard: who's approved, who has a document expiring, who has a low s.

Once it's running, I'll want to connect this to my purchasing pipeline, so the system only lets an order be issued to an approved vendor.

dentro: Vendor + Document (with an expiration) + Rating as linked objects; category/status standardized status
Approval workflow (submit → review → human approval approval)
Document-expiration monitor with automatic renewal chasing + downgrade
Recurring rating (a s per supply) + dashboard by status
fora: Automatic lookup of certificates at government agencies
evol: Blocking purchases from a non-approved vendor (v2, bridges UC-008)

dims.t: Vendor linked to Documents (with an expiration) and Ratings with history; category and approval-status standardized status; spreadsheet import
Vendor list by status (approved/pending/expiring/downgraded) with filters; vendor record with the docs, expirations, and s history
Approval workflow with a human approval gate at approval; durable expiration monitor that chases renewal and downgrades on its own
Checks whether the paperwork is complete for the category, flags an inconsistent/expiring document, summarizes the vendor's performance
Email (connected channel) to chase documents and renewals from the vendor; WhatsApp as an alternative
role-based permissions by role (whoever approves ≠ whoever registers); trail of every approval and downgrade; documents stored under access control

UC-017.kpi: % of active vendors with paperwork in order (no expired document slipping by) + expired documents caught before buying. In the end, you'll say: "I only buy from ones that are eligible, the paperwork doesn't expire on my watch, and I know who delivers well."

UC-018.dor_hook: My internal policies turn into a PDF nobody signs — and I have no way to prove who accepted what.
UC-018.problema: I have internal policies — code of conduct, data-privacy, security, equipment use — but in practice they live in a PDF nobody reads and nobody signs. When something goes wrong, or when a client/auditor asks "was everyone trained on this?", I have no way to prove who read and accepted what, or which version. And when a non-conformity happens (someone breaks a rule), there's no place to log it, work it, and show it was resolved — it's all word of mouth. I'd want a place where my policies are published by version, each person accepts and that gets recorded, and where I open and track non-conformities through to closure — in a way I can show an auditor without cringing.
UC-018.pedido:
I run a company of about 50 employees, and my internal policies (code of conduct, data privacy, information security, equipment use) are PDFs nobody signs today. My problem is that I can't prove who read and accepted each one, or handle it properly when someone breaks a rule. I'll start by loading the policies I already have and the employee list (name, department, role).

I want to publish each policy and request a sign-off from whoever needs to accept it. The system sends the request, the person reads and accepts, and that gets recorded — who accepted, which policy, which version, when. When I update a policy, it becomes a new version and the system asks for the sign-off again from whoever needs it; I want to see who has already accepted the current version and who's still pending, and chase the stragglers on its own.

I also want to log non-conformities: when someone breaks a rule or an incident happens, I (or a manager) open a record, assign an owner and an action plan, attach the evidence, and track it through to closure — with approval from whoever needs to approve the sign-off. And I want a dashboard: sign-offs up to date by policy, open non-conformities, and the history of everything, in a way I can present in an audit.

Once it's running, I'll want it to reopen the sign-off campaign on its own every year and alert me when a policy is "overdue" for review.

dentro: Versioned policy + Sign-off (per person and version) + Non-conformity + Action plan
Sign-off campaign (request → reading → recorded sign-off) + chasing the pending ones
Non-conformity handling (open → action → evidence → closure with human approval)
Dashboard of sign-offs up to date + open NCs + auditable history
fora: Training/quiz with a s inside the flow
Electronic signature with external legal validity
evol: External whistleblowing channel (v2, public page bridge)

dims.t: **Versioned policy linked to Sign-offs (a sign-off tied to the exact version) and to Non-conformities with an action plan and evidence — history and integrity across objects**; status standardized status
Employee-facing surface to read and accept; manager dashboard of sign-offs by policy/version and NCs by status
Durable sign-off campaign with chasing of pending ones; NC-handling workflow (open → action → verification → closure) with an human approval gate
Helps classify the NC, suggests an action plan by type, summarizes compliance status, reminds about the pending ones
E-mail (connected channel) for sign-off requests and chasers; WhatsApp as an alternative
**Tamper-proof trail of who accepted which version when (audit trail) + NC evidence — audit-ready**; role-based permissions by role; masked PII

UC-018.kpi: % of sign-offs up to date on the current version of the policies + non-conformities handled and closed with a trail (vs. staying in word of mouth). In the end, you'll say: "I can prove who accepted what and show my resolved non-conformities in an audit, without the scramble".

UC-019.dor_hook: My metrics only get attention once the problem has already grown — and I still depend on remembering to act.
UC-019.problema: I have metrics that matter — customer response time, conversion rate, stock on an item, delivery delays — but I only look at them when I remember, and when one gets worse I find out late and, on top of that, I have to remember to take action. The cycle "notice the problem → decide what to do → do it" depends entirely on me paying attention. I'd want someone watching these numbers around the clock and, when one of them goes out of range, already taking the agreed action — the small ones on its own, the bigger ones asking me for the OK — and then telling me whether it worked. That way the problem gets handled when it happens, not when I notice.
UC-019.pedido:
I run a services operation with a few metrics I need to keep under control, but today I only react once the problem has already grown. I'll start by registering the metrics I track and their acceptable thresholds (for example: average first response time to a customer within 2h; items in stock above the reorder point; late deliveries below 5%). I can feed these numbers by spreadsheet or manual entry to begin with.

I want a system that watches these metrics all the time and, when one goes out of range, already takes the action I define — not just send me an alert. For example: if response time blows past the limit, it reinforces the queue and notifies the owner; if an item drops below the reorder point, it opens the replenishment; if deliveries fall behind, it triggers the communication protocol with the affected customers.

The important part: I want to dial the autonomy. The small, reversible actions it does on its own and logs for me. The higher-impact ones (changing a price, canceling something, spending above a threshold) it prepares and asks me for the OK first. After acting, I want it to track whether the metric came back to normal and tell me whether the action fixed it or needs to escalate. And I want to see all of this on a dashboard: what fired, what was done, whether it worked.

Once it's running, I'll want to wire the metrics straight from my systems (instead of typing them) and gradually loosen the reins on the actions it has already proven it gets right.

dentro: Metrics + thresholds + incidents/actions as linked objects; feeding by spreadsheet/manual entry
Continuous monitoring; firing a corrective action when the threshold is crossed
Graduated autonomy (small action on its own, high-impact action with human approval) + effect verification
Dashboard: what fired, what was done, whether it worked; trail of the autonomous actions
fora: Automatic reading of metrics from external systems
evol: **External-system metrics via system connectors (v2; full value depends on this)**
Learning the autonomy threshold from a track record of correct calls (v2)

dims.t: Metric linked to a threshold, an incident and an action with history (the value's series over time); severity/status standardized status
Metrics dashboard with a band (ok/warning/breached); incident history with the action taken and the outcome
**Continuous monitoring that fires the corrective workflow when the threshold is crossed; chained action with an human approval gate on the higher-impact ones**

**Continuous monitoring with graduated autonomy 24/7 — decides the corrective action, executes the reversible ones on its own, escalates the high-impact ones and verifies whether the metric came back**
Channels (connected channel) for the actions that communicate; **reading a metric from an external system needs system connectors **
Trail of every autonomous action (essential: the agent acted on its own); autonomy guardrails by impact; role-based permissions; masked PII

UC-019.kpi: time between a metric going out of range and the corrective action happening (from hours/days — when I notice — to minutes, autonomous) + % of incidents resolved without manual intervention. In the end, you'll say: "my numbers are watched and corrected on their own; I only step in for the high-impact decisions".

UC-020.dor_hook: I lose contacts that would ripen over months because I don't have the bandwidth to keep up with each one over time.
UC-020.problema: I have a bunch of contacts who aren't ready to buy now but will be in three, six months — and I simply lose those people because I don't have the bandwidth to stay on top of each one over time. I make one contact, then forget, and by the time I remember the moment has passed. Long-term nurturing, on paper, everyone talks about it; in practice, it's the first thing to fall when the day gets tight. I'd want a sequence that keeps running on its own for months — the right touch at the right time — without depending on my memory, and that stops the moment the person reacts (replied, asked for a proposal), handing me the baton only when it turns into a hot opportunity.
UC-020.pedido:
I have a list of about 300 contacts who showed interest but aren't ready to close now — they'll ripen over months. My problem is that I can't keep in touch over time: I make one touch, forget, and lose the person. I'll start by loading a spreadsheet with these contacts (name, e-mail, company, where they came from, interest, date of last contact).

I want to set up nurturing sequences that run on their own for months. For example: for someone who downloaded a resource, a sequence of touches over 90 days (useful content, an invitation, a case, an offer) spaced so it doesn't wear them out. The most important part: this sequence can't "drop" — it has to keep running week after week even without me touching anything, and pick back up on its own if something fails midway.

When a contact reacts (replies, clicks asking for a proposal, books a call), I want the sequence to stop for them automatically and the contact to reach me as a hot opportunity, with the history of what they've already received — so I don't start from scratch or send them back to the top of the belt. And nothing disappears: if someone asks to opt out, they're removed and it's logged.

Once it's running, I'll want to create a second, different sequence for another type of contact and move people between them as their interest changes.

dentro: Imported contacts + each one's state in the sequence
Durable sequence over weeks/months with spacing, that doesn't drop and recovers after a failure
Automatic exit on reaction → becomes a hot opportunity with history (human approval)
Opt-out logged; e-mail as the channel
fora: Firing across multiple coordinated channels (e-mail + WhatsApp + SMS)
Visual journey editor with complex branching

dims.t: Contact linked to the sequence and to its state in it (which touch, when the next one); spreadsheet import; stage standardized status
List of contacts by sequence stage; sequence editor (touches and spacing); view of who reacted
**Durable sequence over weeks/months that advances on its own, doesn't drop and recovers after a failure; automatic exit on reaction; human approval gate on delivering the hot opportunity**
Helps compose each touch in the right tone; decides when the contact has "reacted" enough to become an opportunity
E-mail (connected channel) for the touches; (evolution) WhatsApp as a 2nd channel
Contacts' PII masked; opt-out respected and logged; trail of what each contact received

UC-020.kpi: hot opportunities generated from the "cold" base that was being lost for lack of follow-up + % of the base actually nurtured with no manual effort. In the end, you'll say: "I stopped losing contacts to forgetfulness; the belt runs on its own for months and hands me the person once they warm up".

UC-021.dor_hook: I get big lists to clean up and it's all by hand; if it stalls midway, someone starts over from scratch.
UC-021.problema: Every so often I get a big list to clean up — a list of thousands of contacts, a batch of records from another system, a messy product spreadsheet — and I need to clean it (remove duplicates, standardize), classify it, and get it in. Today that's a manual grind: someone spends hours in Excel, and if the computer crashes or the person makes a mistake midway, they start over from scratch without knowing what they'd already done. It's tedious, slow and error-prone. I'd want to throw the whole batch at the system and have it process on its own — clean, standardize, classify — showing me the progress, working the doubtful cases with me, and, if it stops midway for any reason, resuming from where it was without reprocessing everything.
UC-021.pedido:
In my work I get, now and then, a big list to clean up before I can use it — for example, a spreadsheet with about 8,000 contacts from a trade show or another system, with inconsistent columns, duplicates, phone numbers in different formats and no classification. Today that's done by hand, takes hours, and if it stalls midway the person starts over without knowing what they've already done.

I want to throw the whole batch at the system and have it process on its own.
