Durable sign-off campaign that chases down stragglers for weeks; non-conformance handling flow with an approval gate on closing critical cases
Classifies the severity of the non-conformance, suggests owner/deadline, helps draft the policy from an industry template
Existing communication channels (e-mail) to chase down sign-offs — no external integration in v1
Tamper-proof trail of the policy in force, sign-offs, and handling (audit proof); employee data protected; human approval on closing critical cases

UC-059.kpi: time to be audit-ready (from days piecing together screenshots and spreadsheets to minutes presenting the trail) + % of sign-offs up to date. In the end, you'll say: "if an audit hits tomorrow, I show everything organized and proven in one click".

objecoes.q: Does this actually make me LGPD-compliant?
objecoes.a: It organizes your policies, sign-offs, and incidents and keeps the proof of everything. The legal responsibility remains yours; the system gives you the control and the evidence to prove it.

objecoes.q: Can anyone delete or change a record afterward?
objecoes.a: No. Each policy in force, sign-off, and handling is logged in a way no one can tamper with — that's what stands as proof in an audit.

objecoes.q: Do I need to build everything from scratch?
objecoes.a: The idea is exactly the opposite: you adopt a ready-made template for your industry and just add your own data. That template store is a stage that's still to come.

UC-060.dor_hook: My systems don't talk to each other, and someone spends the day copying data from one tool to another by hand.
UC-060.problema: I use a bunch of tools day to day — the sales system, the finance spreadsheet, the support tool — and none of them talk to each other. The result is that there's always someone copying data from one place to another by hand: a sale closes over there, it has to be entered in finance over here; a ticket comes in there, a task has to be opened somewhere else. It's rework, it's slow, and it introduces errors. I don't want to (and don't know how to) program an integration; I wanted to grab something ready-made that would already connect my main system to the rest and kick off the processes automatically.
UC-060.pedido:
I have a services company and I use an outside sales system where my orders get closed. The problem is that, once the sale closes over there, someone has to come in by hand and enter the customer and the order into my internal system, create the invoice, and notify the delivery team. It jams up, it's slow, and sometimes they forget.

I want to connect my sales system to my internal system so that, every time an order closes over there, it comes in here on its own: creates the customer if they don't exist, records the order, kicks off the invoicing sequence, and opens the delivery task — without anyone retyping. When the data on one side doesn't match the other (a customer with no tax ID, an odd amount), I want it to set that aside for me to check before moving on, instead of propagating the error.

I don't know how to program an integration and I don't want to depend on a technician every time. I wanted this connection to come ready-made for my sales system, with the process on top, so all I'd need to do is authorize access and map the fields.

Once it's running, I want to be able to connect my support tool too, so tickets turn into tasks here the same way.

dentro: Ready-made durable workflows (order → customer → invoice → task), with a discrepancy queue and human review
Configurable field mapping between the external system and the internal one
Handling of data conflicts/discrepancies before propagating

**Publishing the integration app to the store for third parties to install**

Write-back to the external system + a second connector (v2)

Matches the external order to the internal customer; identifies and categorizes discrepancies for human review
**system connectors (OAuth) for the external sales system, operating over live data; it's the real source of the integration**
Controlled access authorization to the external system; audit trail for every sync; human review before propagating divergent data

UC-060.kpi: hours/week of manual data entry eliminated between the systems + transcription errors avoided. In the end, you'll say: "I stopped having people copy orders from one system to another; they come in on their own and correct".

objecoes.q: Do I need to know how to program to connect my systems?
objecoes.a: No — the idea is to connect with a simple setup of access and field mapping. But that direct connection is still being built; today it isn't ready.

objecoes.q: If the data comes in wrong from there, does it spread the error here?
objecoes.a: No. When the data on one side doesn't match the other, it sets it aside for you to check before moving on.

objecoes.q: Will it duplicate my orders or customers?
objecoes.a: The system matches the external order to the customer that already exists here before creating anything, precisely so it doesn't duplicate.

UC-061.dor_hook: I built a prototype that looks great in the demo, but I can't get it actually running in my business.
UC-061.problema: I had a good idea for my business and managed to build a prototype that works — in the demo it does everything just right and everyone loved it. The problem is when it's time to actually use it: it can't handle the day-to-day, it doesn't run on its own, there's no control over who does what, and when I need to change something I'm afraid of breaking everything or losing what's already there. I invested time, I proved the idea is worth it, but I got stuck: I have a pretty demo and no real operation. I don't want to start over from scratch or become a programmer — I want what I already thought through and the data I've already gathered to turn into a system that actually runs my business.
UC-061.pedido:
I built a prototype of my system in a code builder and it works fine in a demo. It handles my orders and customers: it has a registration screen, a list of orders with statuses (new, in production, delivered), and some buttons that change the status. I also have some real data I've already entered there — about 80 customers and about 200 orders — that I don't want to lose.

The problem is that this is just a pretty shell: it doesn't run on its own, it doesn't nudge anyone, anyone can touch anything, and when I try to change something I break the rest. I don't need you to reuse what's built there — I want you to understand what this system should do and the data it has, and build it again, properly, a version that actually operates my business.

I want it to bring my 80 customers and 200 orders in without me retyping, to keep an eye on the orders and nudge me on its own when an order sits stuck in a status for too long, and to let each person touch only what's theirs. And above all, I want to be able to change the app later — change a status, add a field, change a rule — with it already running and without losing anything that's inside it.

dentro: Extract the **intent** (what the prototype should do) from the owner's description
Migrate the prototype's real **data** (customers, orders) into the re-generated app
Re-generate an **operable** app: runs on its own, chases down stalls, controls access, evolves without losing data
fora: **Importing, wrapping, or running the prototype's CODE (hard rule — anti-moat)**
Automatic batch migration of multiple prototypes
evol: Assisted intent detection from a structured export of the prototype (v2)

dims.t: Model re-generated from the intent (Customer, Order, status as a business standardized status) + the real data migrated without loss
The screens the operation calls for (order list by status, record view, registration) — generated by the platform, not copied from the prototype
What the prototype was missing: it tracks orders and nudges/alerts on its own when an order sits stuck in a status for too long (durable)
Interprets the prototype's intent and the evolution request in natural language; decides when to chase down a stall
Existing channels for the alerts/nudges (e-mail/WhatsApp) — no external integration in v1
Control over who touches what (the prototype had none); evolving the app while it runs **without losing the migrated data** — the proof that the cliff has been solved

UC-061.kpi: prototype → real operation — the owner who had only a demo now has an app that runs the business, with the data migrated and evolving by conversation. In the end, you'll say: "what was a pretty demo became the system that runs my business, and I can change it without breaking anything".

objecoes.q: Will I lose what I've already built and the data I've already entered?
objecoes.a: Your data comes along, no retyping. What changes is that the system is built again, properly, to actually operate — instead of reusing the demo's shell.

objecoes.q: Why not reuse what's already built in the prototype?
objecoes.a: Because what stalls in the prototype is exactly what's underneath. Rebuilding from your intent is what makes it run on its own, with control and room to evolve.

objecoes.q: Will I be able to change it later without breaking everything?
objecoes.a: Yes — that's the difference: you change the app while it's already running, without losing anything that's inside it.

UC-062.dor_hook: I don't know whether my numbers are above or below my industry — I compare on gut feel, with nothing to go on.
UC-062.problema: I run my business in the dark relative to the market. I know my numbers — how much I sell, how long I take to serve a customer, how many customers I lose — but I have no idea whether that's good or bad for my industry. Is my service time fast or slow compared to others who do what I do? Is my default rate normal or running high? Today I compare on gut feel, or I pay a lot for a generic consulting report that doesn't speak to my size. I wanted to know, on a real basis, where I stand against companies like mine — without, of course, handing my numbers over to anyone in the open.
UC-062.pedido:
I operate my business on the platform and I wanted to understand how I'm doing compared to other companies like mine — same industry, similar size. For example: my average time to serve a customer, my rate of customers who pay late, my percentage of proposals that close. I know my numbers, but I have nothing to compare them against.

I want to authorize my metrics to enter, anonymously, an industry benchmark, and in return see where I stand: whether my service time is above or below the average of those who do what I do, whether my default rate is typical or running high. I'll only agree to this if it's clear exactly what I'm sharing, if it's genuinely anonymous (nobody sees my individual number, and I don't see anyone else's), and if I can turn it off whenever I want.

I want to see this in a simple dashboard: my metrics side by side with the industry range, highlighting where I'm doing well and where I'm behind — so I know where to focus.

dentro: Explicit, revocable, and granular consent per metric
Anonymization/aggregation: each company sees the industry range, never another's individual number
Dashboard: my metrics × the industry range, highlighting where I'm doing well/behind
fora: **The aggregated data network itself (the essence of this UC)**
Named comparison ("company X vs company Y") — forbidden by design
evol: **Consented cross-tenant aggregation via consented data channel**
Finer breakdowns (by region, by size) as the base grows

dims.t: Comparable metrics defined by industry (service time, default rate, close rate); consent record per metric with history
Industry-position dashboard (my metrics × the industry range/percentile); a clear consent screen (what I share, turn on/off)
Recurring collection and aggregation of the consented metrics; recomputing the benchmark as the base evolves
Normalizes metrics across companies of different sizes; computes the industry ranges/percentiles; identifies where the company falls outside the range
A consented cross-tenant data channel operating over the platform's live base (not an external system of record, but third parties' live data under consent)
Explicit, granular, and revocable consent; re-identification-proof anonymization (k-anonymity); audit trail of the consented data's use; no individual number exposed

UC-062.kpi: benchmark adoption (how many companies consent and consult it) + a decision triggered by the gap (the company acted on a metric where it was behind the industry). In the end, you'll say: "I found out my service time was 40% above the industry and went after it".

objecoes.q: Will my numbers be exposed to the competition?
objecoes.a: No. You only enter an anonymous, aggregated benchmark: nobody sees your individual number, and you don't see anyone else's — only the industry range.

objecoes.q: Am I required to take part?
objecoes.a: No. You authorize metric by metric, see exactly what you're sharing, and can turn it off whenever you want.

objecoes.q: Does this work today?
objecoes.a: Not yet. It depends on a consented cross-company data base that's being built; without it, an honest industry benchmark doesn't exist.

UC-063.dor_hook: My app handles sensitive data about real people, and I lose sleep afraid it'll leak or be used wrong.
UC-063.problema: My business handles sensitive data about real people — in my case, patients' health information. It keeps me up at night: if it leaks, if the wrong person accesses it, or if I use the data in a way I wasn't allowed to, it's not just a technical problem — it's the patient's trust, it's my legal responsibility. Today it lives in spreadsheets and WhatsApp, with no control at all over who saw what, and anyone on the team can open everything. I need a system where the sensitive data is genuinely protected, where each person only sees what's within their remit, and where I can prove later who accessed what and what they did — because one day someone is going to ask me that.
UC-063.pedido:
I run a psychology clinic with about 6 practitioners and I need to organize the appointments and the patients' notes. To start, I have a spreadsheet of patients (name, phone, ID number, date of birth) and a loose notebook with the notes from each session.

I want a system where each patient has their own record and session history, with the clinical notes. But the most important point is protection: this is real people's health data. Each practitioner can only see the patients they treat — not their colleagues'. The front desk can see contact details and the schedule, but can't open the clinical notes. The sensitive data (ID number, health information) has to stay protected, including when the system uses intelligence to help me summarize something — I don't want that sensitive data getting exposed anywhere.

And I need to be able to prove later who accessed what and when: if a patient or a regulator asks me who opened that record, I have to have the log, in a way no one can tamper with. No sensitive data can be deleted without leaving a trace.

Once it's running, I want to be able to give the patient access to just their own schedule and documents, without them seeing anything else.

dentro: Patient record + session history with protected health data; ID number/health masked before any intelligence
Role-based access: each practitioner sees only their own patients; the front desk can't open clinical notes
Tamper-proof trail of who accessed/changed what; nothing sensitive deletable without a trace
fora: Certified electronic health record (health regulatory standard)
evol: Granular patient consent over data use (v2)

dims.t: Patient linked to sessions and clinical notes; sensitive structural data (ID number, health); session-type standardized status; versioned history
Patient record and history by role; schedule; distinct surfaces for the practitioner and the front desk
Appointment flow (schedule → see the patient → record → track follow-up) — the durable part isn't the focus; protection is
Targeted help (summarize/organize a note) **operating over masked data** — the intelligence never sees the raw PII
Existing channels for appointment reminders (WhatsApp/e-mail); no external integration in v1
**Health/ID-number data masked before the model; row-level access per practitioner; tamper-proof trail of access and changes; nothing sensitive deletable without a trace**

UC-063.kpi: zero improper exposure — every access traceable and within remit, measured by clean access audits (no out-of-role access) + readiness to respond to a data-subject/regulator request (from days in the dark to minutes with the trail in hand). In the end, you'll say: "I sleep easy — the sensitive data is protected and I can prove who accessed what".

objecoes.q: How do I know my client's sensitive data is truly protected?
objecoes.a: The sensitive data stays protected and is hidden before any intelligence uses it; and each person only accesses what's within their remit.

objecoes.q: Can I prove who accessed a record if I'm asked?
objecoes.a: Yes. Every access and change is logged in a way no one can tamper with — you show who, what, and when.

objecoes.q: Can an employee delete a record without anyone knowing?
objecoes.a: No. Nothing sensitive is deleted without leaving a trace in the trail.

UC-064.dor_hook: Approval here is a screenshot in the WhatsApp group — if it's questioned later, I have no solid proof of who signed off on what.
UC-064.problema: At my company, approving things is an informal mess. An off-book discount, an over-limit expense, a contract — it becomes a message in the WhatsApp group: "can I clear this?", "go ahead". Then afterward nobody really knows who approved it, on what basis, and if someone questions it (a partner, an audit, a client), I have a screenshot at most — which proves nothing solid and gets lost among a thousand messages. Worse: if someone approved it by mistake, there's no clean way to undo it, the damage is already done. I wanted approval to be a serious, recorded thing: who has authority for what, the sign-off with proof of what was decided, and the ability to reverse it if it was approved wrongly.
UC-064.pedido:
I have a services company and I need to organize internal approvals, which today happen by shouting and over WhatsApp. The cases that need approval are: a discount over 10% on a proposal, an expense over $2,000, and signing a new contract. Each has a different authority level: the sales manager approves discounts; expenses over $5,000 and contracts only I (the owner) approve.

When someone needs one of these sign-offs, I want the request to become a record: who requested it, what for, the amount, the justification — and route to the person with the right authority to approve or reject it. The approval has to be recorded solidly: who approved it, when, on the basis of which request, in a way no one can tamper with afterward. I don't want a WhatsApp screenshot serving as proof.

And I need to be able to undo: if someone approves by mistake or on wrong information, I want to reverse the approval, making it clear in the history that it was reversed, by whom, and why — without simply deleting it as if it never happened.

I want to see in a dashboard what's pending approval, by whom, and the history of everything that's already been approved or rejected, so I can account for it at any time.

dentro: Approval request as an object (who, what, amount, justification) routed to the right authority
Approval/rejection recorded tamper-proof (who, when, basis); dashboard of pending items and history
**Undo** an approval with a trace (reversed by whom/why), never delete
fora: ERP integration to execute the sign-off
evol: Richer authority rules (by cost center, by client) — v2
Executing the sign-off in the ERP via a connector (v2)

dims.t: Approval request as an object linked to the item (proposal/expense/contract); authority by type and amount band; approval states; versioned history with reversal
Pending-items queue per approver; the request's record with the justification; auditable history dashboard
Routing the request to the correct authority with a human approval gate; controlled reversal of the decision
Identifies which authority applies by the rule (type + amount band); summarizes the request for the approver to decide
Internal in v1 (notifies the approver over an existing channel); no external integration
**Approval as tamper-proof evidence (who/when/basis); undo with a trace instead of delete; authority as the control over who can do what**

UC-064.kpi: proof of approval in seconds (from "where's the screenshot?" to "here's who approved it, when, and on what basis") + zero orphan approvals (every sign-off with an authority and a trace). In the end, you'll say: "if anyone questions a sign-off, I prove on the spot who authorized it".

objecoes.q: What's the difference from a WhatsApp screenshot?
objecoes.a: Anyone can edit a screenshot and it gets lost. Here the approval is a solid record — who approved it, when, and on what basis — that no one can tamper with.

objecoes.q: If something's approved wrongly, can it be reversed?
objecoes.a: Yes. You undo the approval and it's recorded that it was reversed, by whom, and why — without erasing what happened.

objecoes.q: Can someone skip the approval of whoever has the authority?
objecoes.a: No. The request goes to whoever has the right authority and doesn't move without their sign-off.

UC-065.dor_hook: Every department runs a pile of loose spreadsheets and tools, with no control at all over who changed what.
UC-065.problema: At my company, each department has been fending for itself: sales has its spreadsheet, HR has another, finance has a third, and there are still people using some free little tool nobody really knows anything about. It works on improvisation, but it's a mess: nobody knows who changed what, important data lives on one person's machine, and if someone leaves the company, the knowledge walks out with them. There's no control at all. I never rolled out a real system because it was always expensive, slow, and dependent on IT — and so the department didn't wait and built the spreadsheet. I wanted to bring this into a controlled place, where each person only touches what's theirs and what happens gets logged, without it becoming a months-long project.
UC-065.pedido:
My sales team tracks orders in a shared spreadsheet that's become a mess: everyone edits everything, nobody knows who changed what, and there have been cases of someone deleting data by accident and us only finding out later. The spreadsheet has: customer, contact, order amount, owner, status (in negotiation, closed, lost), and some notes.

I want to replace that spreadsheet with a real app, but without it becoming a months-long IT project. I need it to import the spreadsheet as-is, to let each salesperson touch only their own orders (and see others' only if they're a manager), and to log who changed what and when — so I never again have that "it vanished and I don't know who did it". The manager needs an overview; the salespeople only what's theirs.

I want the switch to be simple: import, define who's who, and start using it right away — without stopping the operation. And to be able to adjust it later (a new status, a new field) without breaking what's already running or losing the history.

dentro: Import the department's spreadsheet as-is; turn it into an app with structured data
Role-based access (each person touches only what's theirs; the manager sees everything); trail of who changed what
Adjusting the app while it runs (new field/status) without stopping the operation or losing history
fora: Automatic migration of dozens of spreadsheets at once
Automatic discovery of parallel tools across the company
evol: Batch onboarding of shadow IT from multiple departments (v2)
Multi-app governance dashboard for the manager (v2)

dims.t: Model imported from the spreadsheet (Order, Customer, owner, status); status standardized status; data that used to live loose now structured
Order list/kanban by role; record view; the manager's overview × the salesperson's restricted view
Day-to-day operation (move status, record); the focus isn't a long workflow — it's the control
Helps structure the messy spreadsheet on import (infer columns, clean up)
Internal in v1; existing channels for notifications; no external integration
**Role-based access (what the spreadsheet lacked); trail of who changed what; no data vanishing without a trace; evolve without losing history**

UC-065.kpi: shadow IT eliminated with control — the loose spreadsheet becomes a governed app, measured by in-role access (no "everyone edits everything") + lost-data incidents at zero. In the end, you'll say: "the spreadsheet Wild West is over — each person touches only what's theirs and I know what happened".

objecoes.q: Won't replacing the spreadsheet turn into a huge IT project?
objecoes.a: The idea is the opposite: import the spreadsheet as-is, define who's who, and start using it — without stopping the operation.

objecoes.q: Will I lose what I already have in the spreadsheet?
objecoes.a: No. The spreadsheet imports as-is, with the history, and from then on who touches what gets logged.

objecoes.q: Will my team be able to see only what's theirs?
objecoes.a: Yes. Each person touches only what's theirs; the manager has the overview. It's the control an open spreadsheet doesn't give.

UC-066.dor_hook: When an external audit arrives, I spend days piecing together screenshots and spreadsheets to prove what happened.
UC-066.problema: Every time an external audit arrives — from my big client, from a certification, from a regulator — I panic. I spend days digging through WhatsApp screenshots, e-mails, spreadsheets, and system logs to assemble the "proof" that the operation happened the way it should have: that the process was followed, that the approval existed, that no one touched what they shouldn't have. And the worst part is that this proof is fragile: a screenshot, a spreadsheet — anyone can edit it. The auditor knows this and is suspicious. I wanted my system to already keep everything that matters in a way that no one — not even me — can tamper with, so that at audit time I just present it, instead of reconstructing it in a scramble.
UC-066.pedido:
My company provides services to large clients who audit us periodically, and I need to prove that the operation followed what was agreed. Today I track deliveries in a spreadsheet (client, delivery, owner, promised date, completed date, who checked it) and the proof of all this is fragile.

I want a system where every important event in the process is logged automatically and in a way no one can alter afterward: when the delivery was made, by whom, who checked it, whether there was a delay and why. No "fixing up" the spreadsheet before the audit — the record has to be solid and chronological, and if someone tries to tamper with it, that has to become evident.

At audit time, I want to be able to generate a report of the trail for a given period or a specific client, showing the sequence of what happened, so I can simply present it to the auditor — instead of spending days piecing together screenshots. I also want to be able to give the auditor read-only access to the trail, without them changing anything.

Once it's running, I want to add more types of event to what's recorded, without losing what's already logged.

dentro: Automatic, tamper-proof logging of the process's events (done/checked/delayed, by whom, when)
Detection of evident tampering attempts (the trail flags it if touched)
Trail report by period/client + read-only access for the auditor
fora: Certification/signature by an external authority (notary, PKI)
evol: External anchoring of the trail for independent proof (v2)
Consolidating the trail across integrated apps (v2)

dims.t: Trail event linked to the item (delivery/client), with author, timestamp, and chronological chaining; event-type standardized status
Chronological view of the trail; report generator by period/client; read-only access for the auditor
Automatic logging of events as the operation happens (the durable part isn't the focus; tamper-proofing is)
Minimal — the trail is recorded fact, not inference (the value is in NOT being "the system's guesswork")
Export/present to the auditor; external read-only access; no inbound integration in v1
**Tamper-evident trail as a condition of the sale; chaining that flags any alteration; read-only access for a third-party auditor**

UC-066.kpi: audit-prep time (from days piecing together screenshots to minutes generating the trail report) + audit accepted with no reservation about the integrity of the proof. In the end, you'll say: "when an audit arrives, I present — I don't reconstruct — and the auditor trusts the proof".

objecoes.q: How does the auditor know I didn't edit the trail beforehand?
objecoes.a: The trail is chained and flags any tampering attempt — you can't 'fix it up' before the audit without it becoming evident.

objecoes.q: Will the auditor change my data?
objecoes.a: No. You give them read-only access to the trail; they review it and change nothing.

objecoes.q: Do I need to build the report by hand?
objecoes.a: No. You generate the trail report by period or client and present it — instead of spending days piecing together screenshots.

UC-067.dor_hook: It's an operation that can't go wrong, but today I trust to luck and have no way to prove that each step was checked.
UC-067.problema: I have a process in my business that simply can't go wrong — every step has to be exact, because an error here costs dearly, causes losses, or even risk. Today this process depends on people checking by hand, and I trust to luck and to people paying attention. When it goes wrong, I find out late, and I have no way to reconstruct precisely what happened at each stage: who did it, what was checked, whether it matched. I don't want a system that "thinks" everything's fine — in a critical process, guesswork won't do. I want each step checked exactly and logged, in a way no one can alter, proving that the check was done and came out right.
UC-067.pedido:
I run a shipping operation for high-value orders that can't go wrong: each assembled order has to match exactly what was bought before it leaves, or I ship the wrong thing and the loss is big. The process is: the order comes in (items and quantities), the team picks it, and before dispatch someone checks whether what was picked matches the order.

I want the system to run this process so the check is exact, not an "I think it's right". For each order, it compares item by item what was picked against what was ordered — by code and quantity — and only releases dispatch if it matches 100%. If it doesn't match, it locks and shows exactly what's extra or missing, to fix before it leaves. If it's an off-standard case (a substitution, an exception), I want to approve it manually, making it clear that it was an exception.

Every step — picked by whom, checked, the result of the check, released by whom — has to sit in a record that no one can alter afterward, so that if there's a problem with a client, I can prove exactly what happened with that order.

I want a dashboard with the orders at each stage (to pick, checking, locked by discrepancy, released) and the full record of each one.

dentro: Durable flow for the critical order (pick → check → release) with a lock on discrepancy
**Deterministic verification** item by item (code + quantity); releases only on a 100% match
Tamper-proof ledger of each step (who picked/checked/released, the result); exception with approval
fora: Automatic reading by barcode/RFID via hardware
evol: Capture via barcode/RFID reader (v2, device connector)
Pull the order and draw down inventory in the ERP via a connector (v2)

dims.t: Order linked to items and to the check; integrity rule (releases only on an exact match); event ledger; stage standardized status and discrepancy-type standardized status
Dashboard by stage (to pick/checking/locked/released); check screen that shows what's extra/missing; record per order
Durable workflow (pick → check → release) with a lock on discrepancy and resume; approval gate on exceptions
Proposes the item-by-item match and categorizes the discrepancy — but the **verdict is deterministic** (the rule confirms; the agent doesn't "decide that it matches")
Existing channels to flag a lock/exception; hardware-based reading is left to v2
**Deterministic verification (structural anti-hallucination) + tamper-proof ledger as proof of each step; release only on an exact match; exception traced**

UC-067.kpi: critical errors avoided (discrepancies locked before dispatch that would have become losses) + proof per order (reconstructing exactly what happened with any order). In the end, you'll say: "nothing goes out wrong because the check is exact, and I prove every step of any order".

objecoes.q: Can I trust it won't release a wrong order?
objecoes.a: It only releases when what was picked matches the order 100%, item by item. If it doesn't match, it locks and shows what's extra or missing.

objecoes.q: What if it's a system that 'thinks' it's right?
objecoes.a: It's not guesswork. The check is an exact comparison by code and quantity; the system proposes, but the rule is what confirms.

objecoes.q: Can I prove what happened if a client disputes it?
objecoes.a: Yes. Every step — who picked, checked, and released it — sits in a record no one can alter, order by order.
